quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Actual yield"?

options.text: The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally
Electrons fill orbitals in order of increasing energy, from lowest to highest
Loss of electrons; occurs at the anode; oxidation state increases
If x/[initial] < 5%, the approximation [HA] ≈ [HA]₀ is valid; avoids solving the quadratic equation for weak acid/base equilibria

quiz_questions.explanation: "Actual yield" is defined as: The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Atom economy"?

options.text: The mass of one mole of a substance in grams per mole (g/mol); numerically equal to the atomic/molecular mass in amu
A strong dipole-dipole interaction between H bonded to N, O, or F and a lone pair on another N, O, or F atom
(Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction
Ka1 >> Ka2 >> Ka3; each successive proton is harder to remove because it leaves a more negatively charged anion

quiz_questions.explanation: "Atom economy" is defined as: (Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Green chemistry"?

options.text: Equilibrium constant for weak acid dissociation: HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻; larger Ka = stronger acid
Concentration in moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); the most common concentration unit in chemistry
The design of chemical products and processes that minimize the use and generation of hazardous substances
An equation relating reaction rate to reactant concentrations: rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ; exponents determined experimentally

quiz_questions.explanation: "Green chemistry" is defined as: The design of chemical products and processes that minimize the use and generation of hazardous substances. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Side reaction"?

options.text: E = E° - (RT/nF)ln Q; adjusts cell potential for non-standard concentrations; at equilibrium E = 0 and Q = K
The rate at a specific moment, determined by the slope of the tangent to the concentration-vs.-time curve
The attraction between an ion and a polar molecule; the dominant force when ionic compounds dissolve in water
An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product

quiz_questions.explanation: "Side reaction" is defined as: An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: In the context of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency, which best defines "Percent yield"?

options.text: The distribution of molecular kinetic energies at a given temperature; a higher temperature shifts the peak and increases the fraction of molecules above Ea
k = Ae^(-Ea/RT); relates the rate constant to temperature, activation energy, and the frequency/orientation factor A
(Actual yield / Theoretical yield) × 100%; values < 100% result from side reactions, loss, or incomplete reaction
The number of possible arrangements of particles and energy; related to entropy by Boltzmann equation: S = k ln W

quiz_questions.explanation: In Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency, "Percent yield" refers to: (Actual yield / Theoretical yield) × 100%; values < 100% result from side reactions, loss, or incomplete reaction.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The amount of product actually obtained from a reaction; measured experimentally"?

options.text: Kinetic molecular theory
ΔG° = -nFE°
Actual yield
State function

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Actual yield".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "(Mass of desired product / Total mass of all products) × 100%; measures efficiency of atom utilization in a reaction"?

options.text: Formal charge
Selective precipitation
Atom economy
Half-reaction method

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Atom economy".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "The design of chemical products and processes that minimize the use and generation of hazardous substances"?

options.text: Diprotic acid
Standard free energy of formation
Conjugate pair
Green chemistry

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Green chemistry".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "An unwanted reaction that competes with the desired reaction, consuming reactants and reducing the yield of the target product"?

options.text: Side reaction
Pressure/volume change
Equilibrium vs. completion
Temperature change

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Side reaction".

quiz_questions.question_text: In Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency, which term matches: "(Actual yield / Theoretical yield) × 100%; values < 100% result from side reactions, loss, or incomplete reaction"?

options.text: Ka (acid dissociation constant)
Percent yield
Atomic number (Z)
Kp vs. Kc

quiz_questions.explanation: In Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency, this refers to "Percent yield".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency is accurate?

options.text: Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
K > 1 favors products; K < 1 favors reactants.
Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency quantify how productive a real reaction is compared to the theoretical maximum.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Strong acid–strong base titrations have equivalence point at pH 7.
Percent yield is always ≤ 100% due to side reactions, incomplete reactions, loss during transfer, or impure reactants.
For aA + bB → products, rate = k[A]ᵐ[B]ⁿ, where m and n are reaction orders (determined experimentally, NOT from coefficients).
Amino acids are polyprotic with both acidic (COOH) and basic (NH₂) groups, existing as zwitterions at physiological pH.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Atom economy measures the fraction of reactant atoms incorporated into the desired product.
Atoms consist of protons (p⁺, +1 charge, ~1 amu) and neutrons (n⁰, neutral, ~1 amu) in the nucleus, surrounded by electrons (e⁻, -1 charge, ~1/1836 amu) in orbitals.
Ion-dipole forces are the strongest IMF and govern dissolution of ionic compounds in polar solvents.
This allows calculation of ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly, by combining known reactions.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Green chemistry aims to maximize both percent yield and atom economy while minimizing waste and hazardous byproducts.
The relative strength of an acid is inversely related to the strength of its conjugate base.
Equilibrium Constants quantify the position of equilibrium.
Entropy increases when: solids melt or liquids vaporize, gases expand, temperature increases, or the number of particles increases.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency quantify how productive a real reaction is compared to the theoretical maximum.
For redox reactions, the half-reaction method balances electron transfer in acidic or basic solutions.
Entropy (S) measures the number of possible microstates (disorder).
A valid mechanism must: (1) have elementary steps that sum to the overall equation, (2) be consistent with the experimentally determined rate law, and (3) have each step be physically reasonable.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Industrial process design using Hess's Law to predict energy requirements for multi-step chemical syntheses without performing every reaction experimentally
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Pharmaceutical synthesis often involving multi-step reactions where overall yield is the product of individual step yields: 5 steps at 80% each = 32.8% overall
Forensic chemistry using stoichiometric analysis to determine the composition of unknown substances from mass spectral data

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Green chemistry principles in industry: catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes (100% atom economy) preferred over reactions producing significant waste byproducts
Ocean buffering against acidification: carbonate/bicarbonate system absorbing excess CO₂, but capacity being overwhelmed by current emission rates
Predicting reaction feasibility in industrial processes: ΔG calculations determine whether a proposed reaction path is thermodynamically viable before investing in equipment

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Diamond-to-graphite conversion: thermodynamically spontaneous (ΔG < 0) but kinetically extremely slow—diamonds are 'metastable' and effectively permanent
Blood pH maintenance at 7.35-7.45: even small deviations cause acidosis (7.45), both life-threatening conditions
Dissolution of ammonium nitrate: endothermic (ΔH > 0) but spontaneous because the large increase in entropy (ΔS >> 0) from ions dispersing in solution makes ΔG < 0
Quality control in manufacturing: monitoring percent yield to detect equipment problems, contaminated reagents, or process deviations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency?

options.text: Environmental chemistry: calculating the enthalpy of acid rain formation from SO₂ emissions using a series of known thermochemical equations
Household acids and bases: vinegar (acetic acid, Ka = 1.8×10⁻⁵), lemon juice (citric acid), baking soda (NaHCO₃ acts as a weak base)
Pharmaceutical synthesis often involving multi-step reactions where overall yield is the product of individual step yields: 5 steps at 80% each = 32.8% overall
Aluminum production by Hall-Héroult process: electrolysis of molten Al₂O₃ in cryolite at ~960°C; consumes ~5% of US electricity production

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency in practice?

options.text: Catalytic converters using Pt, Pd, and Rh heterogeneous catalysts to lower Ea for converting exhaust pollutants (CO, NOₓ) to harmless products
Refrigerators and air conditioners: external work (electricity) is required to decrease entropy locally (cool the interior) while increasing total universal entropy
Lithium-ion batteries: rechargeable cells using Li⁺ intercalation in graphite (anode) and metal oxide (cathode); powering smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles
Green chemistry principles in industry: catalytic hydrogenation of alkenes (100% atom economy) preferred over reactions producing significant waste byproducts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Percent Yield and Reaction Efficiency.
